does: This paper introduces Layerwise Convergence Fingerprinting (LCF), a runtime monitoring approach that detects misbehavior in deployed large language models by analyzing the trajectory of hidden states across layers. The method computes Mahalanobis distances on inter-layer differences, applies Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage for robust covariance estimation, and uses leave-one-out calibration on just 200 clean examples to set detection thresholds. The approach requires no reference model, trigger knowledge, or model retraining, making it applicable to opaque third-party models. LCF is evaluated across four model architectures on diverse threats including 56 backdoor variants, three jailbreak techniques, and prompt injection attacks, demonstrating its ability to detect training-time backdoors, safety-alignment subversion, and instruction-override attacks that escape static validation.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in runtime AI safety by extending prior work on out-of-distribution detection and anomaly detection in neural networks to the domain of LLM misbehavior. It responds to limitations in existing runtime defenses that typically assume access to clean reference models or trigger specifications—assumptions violated with proprietary models. The paper builds on statistical anomaly detection methods like Mahalanobis distance-based approaches while adapting them to the multi-layer structure of transformers. It connects to recent research on detecting backdoors, jailbreaks, and prompt injections, which have largely focused on either training-time defenses or methods requiring model knowledge. LCF bridges this gap by treating layer-wise convergence dynamics as an intrinsic health signal, drawing inspiration from interpretability research that views hidden-state trajectories as meaningful indicators of model computation.
enables: This work opens pathways for practical runtime safety of deployed LLMs in high-stakes applications. By requiring only clean calibration data and no model internals, LCF enables organizations to monitor third-party APIs and closed-source models, extending safety guarantees to deployment settings where retraining or white-box inspection are infeasible. The layerwise fingerprinting approach could be extended to detect other forms of distribution shift, model degradation, or adversarial input families not seen during training. Future work could investigate whether these convergence signatures transfer across similar model families, enabling shared defense signatures. The method also suggests directions for adversarial robustness research—developing attacks specifically designed to evade convergence-based monitors—and for interpretability work connecting layer-wise dynamics to model behavior, potentially yielding deeper understanding of when and why models misbehave.
